sourceMovie.title: Split
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Split (2017) stuck because it commits to its horror premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. M. Night Shyamalan's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The horror and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Halloween
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Halloween (2018) is the natural next step after Split: same horror DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and thriller elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Trap
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Split who respond to its horror will find Trap (2024) immediately familiar. Both films share a thriller sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Case 39
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Split fans keep landing on Case 39 (2009) — the horror is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Unsane
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Unsane draws from the same horror well as Split, channeling thriller in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2018 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Haunt
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Haunt (2019) captures the horror appeal of Split without simply imitating it. The thriller streak running through both films is what puts Haunt at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: The Long Walk
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror appeal of Split — that dark edge, the thriller — translates cleanly into The Long Walk (2025). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Psycho II
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Psycho II (1983) clearly understood what makes horror work — the same quality that keeps Split rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Sixth Sense
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Sixth Sense (1999) is the kind of film Split fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The thriller overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Glass
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Glass shares Split's gift for thriller — the 2019 film delivers drama without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Smile
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Split compelling — the horror, the mystery — shows up in Smile (2022) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

faq.question: What should I watch after Split?
faq.answer: Try Halloween, Trap, Case 39—each shares DNA with Split while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Split?
faq.answer: Split (2017) is best described as Horror, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Split worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this horror lane.
